# Transcrição: 07-Site Optimisation- Visitor Polls

- **Fonte:** 07-Site Optimisation- Visitor Polls
- **Duração:** 10:42
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Now one of the most powerful things that you can do as part of your user experience analysis is to ask people whether there's anything stopping them from buying, signing up or whatever your goal is on your key page. So this example here, we're asking people on the landing page for a particular product straight from ads whether there's anything stopping them from buying today. So we don't have a lot of data here but you can at least see on this particular form 69% people said yes, 31% said no. So that immediately tells me that there's something that we need to dive into. Now bear in mind people that answered no, will they buy, will they not buy, you don't know. So that's not something which tells you that 31% should be your conversion rate because that's kind of what it suggests. That's not the case. This tells me that people that answered the poll, which is not everyone, of which 69% said they did have a problem. So that's good for me because I can now see that this particular landing page versus another versus another has potential optimization opportunities. So I want to say has problems but has optimization opportunities. So it's all good and well asking whether they do have a problem but what about the detail and what I like to do is ask the question on the page, is there anything stopping you from X? A simple yes, no response and if they say yes, then ask for some written feedback. Now this is just some of the feedback that we've received on this particular page and it's quite interesting to see what is on people's minds and actually you can see the first response there. Someone's not so keen on seeing the pop up. One of the things you have to do is try and get the timing right. So when we actually launched this, the setting was 20 seconds, which is way too fast because if you land on a page, I would argue, especially the long form landing page that we actually have, if you don't spend more than 30, 40 seconds on the page, you're kind of not engaged anyway. So we recently changed that to 60 seconds. So it gives people a bit more time to actually read the page, absorb the content, scroll down and then decide whether it's kind of right for them. And also by looking at our user replays, we see on average a minute, minute and a half is the time that the most engaged people spend on the page. So that's quite interesting. Now then you kind of look down and see price, too expensive, money, what's the cost price, and seeing the cheaper, elsewhere, etc. And you know, part of this is to understand what the consumer mindset is around the traffic that you're driving and then your ability to convert. Now for example, this particular store is converting quite highly at 4 to 5%. The return on ad spend averages 2 to 300%, which is profitable.

## [03:01 - 06:05]

So you might look at this and say, well, if everything's working fine, then why are so many people talking about price? There's two sides of this. Number one could be our targeting, that we're actually targeting the wrong person because if price is their biggest barrier, then maybe we need to adjust our marketing and find people who can afford it. This is a $50 item, so it's not an absolutely expensive thing. But you know, one side is the traffic. The other side is maybe our messaging in the ad and the landing page is not pushing the value enough. So for example, if someone sees a $50 item and you focus on the functional benefits of that $50 item, it's easy for them to decide whether it's right for them and put a price against it. If you're talking about the outcome and the value and how this is going to change their life, their world, their day, etc., that's harder to put a price on. So the example I like to use is of the LED dog collars, which were quite popular, you know, three, four years ago. The example there is a lot of the sellers were selling it for about $20, $30 and talking about the functional benefits. So it's bright, it keeps your dog visible, etc., etc. Then you had people that were charging $40 to $50 and their angle was that this could save your dog's life. Now I don't think if you are a dog owner that you're going to say necessarily that saving my dog's life is not worth $45, $50, as long as I've pitched the message right and I've built the ads right and the targeting and the landing page, then if I've convinced you that you need this item to save your dog's life potentially, then the price becomes a secondary motive. Like your first motive is, will this actually work for me? Can I see myself experiencing the outcome or in this example, saving my dog's life and therefore is it worth $45, $50? That makes it so much more affordable. You know, if you look at the some of the stats around how much people spend on their pets health versus their own, just give you an example of certain kind of circumstances where the price is not a barrier. So if you're talking about value and expense and things like that, if you pitch it right, then it's not really about reducing the cost or introducing discounts. It's working on your value proposition. So you know, we talk about 5W avatar and the emotional and functional hooks. This is what it's all about because if you've got a great product and you can find your consumer on Facebook, which is infinitely easier than it was three, four, five years ago, and you can pitch your offer right, which is the proposition between the product and the audience, then price shouldn't be a barrier. Now, you know, that's something for you to experiment and play with. What I'm looking for here are kind of clues as to what I can do to improve my page.

## [06:05 - 09:06]

So here, part of it is, you know, I'm thinking about the traffic optimization. And the other part is I'm thinking about the landing page and maybe I need to work harder on the value. So, you know, it could be that my landing page needs improving or it could be that my traffic needs optimizing as well. So that's something which is worth kind of looking at. And you know, like as we've shared before in previous examples, if you tag your campaigns in the right way, you can see which ad set, which audience is actually telling you what. So in this example, for example, you know, I'm looking at my campaign here and I can go and copy and paste that campaign name back into ads manager and see, you know, I could group these up and I can, you can also download these into a spreadsheet. I can start looking at whether particular audiences are actually giving me, you know, more feedback about price and to expensive than others. So this one, for example, is 1% lookalike. It could be that there's something in that lookalike that, for example, like here's an example that 1% lookalike could be working really, really well. But what if we need to add an interest on top just to avoid the people who are, you know, maybe not going to be able to afford it? You know, one of the examples is, let's say, for example, this is a garage like. So if I'm targeting people on a 1% lookalike and it's kind of working okay, but I want to optimize it for people that have a higher income, I could add one or two interests to my lookalike to refine it a bit more and say, right, I want people that are, you know, they like Gucci or they like Mercedes or something like that, some indication that the user is a higher net worth individual, for example. So that's the way you can kind of use this data to trim back and look at how you can optimize your traffic as well. So that's on the kind of data side. And in terms of setup, it's fairly easy. So this is how we do it in hotjar. Again, it will differ for different tools. The main thing is the question. So it's only think preventing you from purchasing at this point. Generally I would kind of change that to today, like making that as focused and poignant as possible. It just that the way you ask the question is really, really important here because is there anything preventing you from doing that purchase today? That's the thing that you want to know. So that's the thing that I would kind of focus in on the question. Simple yes, please tell us well or no, that's fine. Then in the appearance, you want to literally just kind of have it as a kind of subtle pop-up that's going to be there, but not too heavy. So whatever matches your site on mobile, obviously this will take up a lot of real estate at the bottom.

## [09:06 - 10:42]

For desktop, it will be the bottom right or bottom left and you can choose that. Then it's the behavior. So which devices do you want it to run on? For example, you might just want it on desktop because on mobile, you don't want to ruin the experience or maybe just on mobile because that's where your traffic mostly is. Which pages you want it to be triggered on? We would generally have this on the key landing pages. Then you can kind of go through and in particular, I'm looking at the trigger. So here, 60 seconds is what we've got. It might be that we need to modify that and there's a few different options here that you can kind of play on as well. What we also do is on the trigger, only fire it once because if they don't respond, you don't want to keep on kind of pushing it to them because that could frustrate people as well. Then that's literally it. So at that point, you're launching it, you're running it. At the moment, we are collecting 100% of data on this store. You could also edit that because if you've got a high volume e-com store, you could really rack up your responses a bit too fast and maybe you want a week's worth of data. So you can play with limiting that as well. Like I said, the goal here is to make sure that you can find additional insights alongside everything else we're looking at. So heat maps, recordings and polls in particular. These three things are three of the biggest ways that we identify optimization opportunities and then we feedback that into our testing. So whether it's testing on traffic to see if we can improve our targeting, testing back into ads to see if we can test the messaging there or into our landing pages as well.
